title: Choose Suffering Instead of Sin
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 05-19-24 -AM- Choose Suffering Instead of Sin - 1 Peter 4_1-6 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Peter Returns to the Main Theme
subpoints: Chapter 4:1 begins with "therefore" — Peter returns to verse 17 after giving the three examples of vindication
The foreign idea: it is better to suffer than to sin — this cuts against the idol of comfort and ease in our culture
Three points: Christ equips us to obey God (vv. 1-2); we have already wasted enough time sinning (vv. 3-4); God will judge the righteous and unrighteous (vv. 5-6)

outline.title: Christ Equips Us to Obey God (Verses 1-2)
subpoints: Suffering for righteousness' sake means you chose not to sin — the suffering comes from choosing to obey
"Has ceased from sin" does not mean sinless perfection — it means a clear break with sin, a settled pattern of choosing obedience
The more we choose to suffer instead of sinning, the more we are strengthened to resist sin
Christ as example: if Christ who was perfect could entrust Himself to God, how much more should we?
Christ as more than example: we are united to Him — He bore our sins in His own body and by His stripes we were healed
Romans 6:1-4: we have been baptized into Christ's death and raised to walk in newness of life
Two results of union with Christ: freedom from living for the lusts of the flesh, and ability to live for the will of God

outline.title: We Have Already Wasted Enough Time Sinning (Verses 3-4)
subpoints: Peter writes to a church mostly saved out of the Gentile world — they have collectively done enough of the will of the Gentiles
Six characteristics of Gentile life (all self-centered and lacking self-control): lewdness, lusts, drunkenness, revelries, drinking parties, abominable idolatries
Where you came from does not dictate who you are in Christ — "if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation"
In Christ, these people are no longer Gentiles — they are part of a new people, the Israel of God
The world finds it strange when you no longer join them in the flood of dissipation and speaks evil of you — this is expected
The faithful Christian will be condemned by the world because his life is a condemnation to the world
Note: you do not need to have practiced these sins before you can be faithful — those raised in Christ and never in rebellion should praise God

outline.title: God Will Judge the Righteous and the Unrighteous (Verses 5-6)
subpoints: Verse 5: unbelievers who blaspheme you will give account to God who is ready to judge the living and the dead
"The living" and "the dead" — persecutors in this life and in the life to come
Verse 6: the gospel was preached to people who were judged in the flesh by the world, but live in the spirit by God
God vindicates those the world condemns — examples: Christ's resurrection, Noah's flood, our baptism
Application: entrust yourself to God; He will vindicate you; you do not have to worry about it

outline.title: Application: Relationships with Unbelievers
subpoints: In close friendships with unbelievers, there will be pushback for not going along — if there is never any conflict, examine your life
Romantic relationships: a believer living faithfully will have a fundamentally miserable romantic relationship with an unbeliever
"Can two walk together unless they are agreed?" (Amos 3:3) — foundational differences are too great
The pattern of Scripture: when a believer enters an unequal relationship, it is the believer who is brought low, not the unbeliever lifted up

summary:
In this sermon from 1 Peter 4:1-6, Pastor Tito Lyro focuses on the striking claim that it is better to suffer than to sin — a truth that cuts directly against the cultural idol of comfort and ease. Peter returns here to verse 17's teaching, now supported by the three examples of vindication just given (Christ, Noah, baptism), and unfolds three reasons why the Christian should choose suffering over sin.

First, Christ equips us to obey God. Choosing to suffer for righteousness means you have already chosen not to sin, and that moral choice — exercised repeatedly — strengthens the pattern of obedience. The phrase "has ceased from sin" does not mean sinless perfection (Scripture elsewhere rules that out), but a decisive break with sin's controlling power. Christ serves as both example and source: not only did He Himself suffer rather than sin, choosing to entrust Himself to the Father, but believers are united to Him in His death and resurrection. As Romans 6 teaches, they have been baptized into His death and raised to walk in newness of life — which means the power to choose obedience over sin is available through that union.

Second, the church has already wasted enough time living like the surrounding culture. Peter lists six characteristics of Gentile life — lewdness, lusts, drunkenness, revelries, drinking parties, and abominable idolatries — all marked by lack of self-control and self-centeredness. But these people are no longer Gentiles. In Christ they are a new creation, the Israel of God, and what formerly characterized them need not characterize them any longer. The world will find this strange and will speak evil of the faithful Christian — but this is precisely what faithfulness looks like.

Third, God will judge. The unbelievers who blaspheme the faithful for their godly conduct will give account to the Judge of the living and the dead. This brings the passage back to the overarching theme of 1 Peter: entrust yourself to God, He will vindicate you. The sermon closes with a direct application to relationships with unbelievers, particularly romantic relationships, arguing that a believer living faithfully will inevitably experience conflict in such relationships — and if there is none, that is itself a reason to examine one's own faithfulness.
transcript:
## Introduction: Peter Returns to the Main Theme

For the reading of God's Word this morning, we're turning to 1 Peter chapter 4, reading verses 1 through 6.

"Therefore, since Christ suffered for us in the flesh, arm yourselves also with the same mind, for he who has suffered in the flesh has ceased from sin, that he no longer should live the rest of his time in the flesh for the lusts of men, but for the will of God. For we have spent enough of our past lifetime in doing the will of the Gentiles, when we walked in lewdness, lusts, drunkenness, revelries, drinking parties, and abominable idolatries. In regard to these, they think it strange that you do not run with them in the same flood of dissipation, speaking evil of you. They will give an account to Him who is ready to judge the living and the dead. For this reason the gospel was preached also to those who are dead, that they might be judged according to men in the flesh, but live according to God in the spirit."

Let us pray together. Father in heaven, we thank you for your Word. We pray that you would speak to us through it today. We pray that the words of our mouth might be to the edification of your people. In Jesus's name. Amen. Please be seated.

When we come to chapter 4 verse 1, we start with the word "therefore," and here Peter returns to where he had stopped before giving us the three examples of God's vindication of His people — Christ being vindicated by the resurrection, Noah being vindicated by the waters of the flood, and our vindication through our baptism. And now he goes back and reaches back to verse 17 where he taught us that it is better to suffer than to sin.

This is a foreign idea to our culture where comfort and ease are to be aspired to above all else. This is one of the big idols of our culture: if it hurts, it's not good; if it's difficult, don't do it; if it's hard, watch TV instead. Ease and comfort seem to be a big idol of our culture. And we find this idea that comfort and ease are some sort of supreme good even in the church of Jesus Christ.

What does it mean that it is better to feel uncomfortable than to sin? Well, Peter says that if it is for the sake of living righteously, the answer is yes — it is better to suffer than to sin.

I want us to consider three things this morning. First, Christ equips us to obey God — that's in verses 1 and 2. Second, we have already wasted enough time sinning — that's in verses 3 and 4. And thirdly, because of these two things, let us not waste any more time sinning because God will judge the righteous and the unrighteous — that's in verses 5 and 6. After we consider these three thoughts, I want to put before you an application of this truth as far as relationships are concerned.

## Christ Equips Us to Obey God (Verses 1-2)

Look at verses 1 and 2: "Therefore, since Christ suffered for us in the flesh, arm yourselves also with the same mind, for he who has suffered in the flesh has ceased from sin, that he no longer should live the rest of his time in the flesh for the lusts of men, but for the will of God."

Suffering in the context of 1 Peter is suffering brought upon a follower of Jesus Christ because he or she is faithfully believing and doing what God says in His word — what Peter refers to as "the will of God." That's what suffering is in this context: what is happening to you because you believe and do what God calls you to believe and do.

If a Christian is suffering for righteousness' sake, he or she is doing so because he or she chose not to sin. If you chose to sin, you would not suffer for righteousness' sake — it's as simple as that. But if you're suffering for righteousness' sake, it is because you have chosen not to sin. You have chosen to suffer instead of sinning, otherwise you would not be suffering as Peter defines suffering.

The phrase "has ceased from sin" cannot mean no longer sins at all, because we know that is not true of people who actually have suffered for righteousness' sake — they still sin after that. The Bible says that no one in this life can achieve a perfect state of moral obedience. As 1 John 1:8 says, if we say that we have no sin, we deceive ourselves and the truth is not in us. And James says for we all stumble in many things.

What does Peter mean? What it means is that the person who is suffering for doing what is right has made a clear break with sin. They are suffering because they chose not to sin. They have most definitely acted in a way which shows that obeying God — not avoiding hardships — is the most important motivation for their life.

So following through with a decision to obey God, even when it will mean physical or emotional or social suffering, has a morally strengthening effect in our lives. It is exercising unto godliness, as Paul speaks of in 1 Timothy 4:7. The more we choose to suffer instead of sinning, the more we are going to be able to stop sinning and take the suffering that comes from obeying God. This exercise commits us more firmly than ever before to a pattern of action where obedience is even more important than our desire to avoid pain.

Here Peter offers the fact that Christ Himself suffered in the flesh for doing what is right as an encouragement for our persevering in suffering for doing what is right. How is Christ an encouragement in this way? Well, if Christ who was absolutely perfect was able to entrust Himself to God, how much more should we? If anyone could make the case that they had the right to retaliate for evil done against them, that would be Jesus. If anyone had the right to say, "I don't deserve to suffer," that would be Jesus. And what did He do? Like a sheep going to the slaughter, He submitted. So we can arm ourselves with Christ's example and choose to suffer instead of sinning.

Brothers and sisters, nobody can make us do anything — period. Nobody can make us. You say, "Well, but what if they put a gun to my head?" No — you still have a choice, don't you? Nobody can make us do anything. So Peter encourages us: let's do what God calls us to do and take whatever consequences come from that, even suffering in the hands of an unbelieving world.

Peter often puts Christ as example in this area. If you look back at chapter 2 verse 21: "For to this you were called, because Christ also suffered for us, leaving us an example, that you should follow His steps." But He is more than an example — we are also united to Him. Notice how quickly Peter moves from Christ being an example to our union with Him in chapter 2 verses 22 through 24, quoting Isaiah 53: "Who committed no sin, nor was deceit found in His mouth; who, when He was reviled, did not revile in return; when He suffered, He did not threaten, but committed Himself to Him who judges righteously" — and then he says: "Who Himself bore our sins in His own body on the tree, that we, having died to sins, might live for righteousness — by whose stripes you were healed."

Do you hear that? You were healed from sinning by Christ's stripes. So we arm ourselves with the example of Christ, but we supremely arm ourselves with our union with Christ, through which we died to sin.

Verse 2 indicates that Peter has this idea in mind, because the language here parallels the language Paul uses in Romans 6, even using baptism like Peter just did in chapter 3. Romans 6:1-4: "What shall we say then? Shall we continue in sin that grace may abound? Certainly not! How shall we who died to sin live any longer in it? Or do you not know that as many of us as were baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into His death? Therefore we were buried with Him through baptism into death, that just as Christ was raised from the dead by the glory of the Father, even so we also should walk in newness of life."

Peter lists two results of our union with Christ in verse 2: we don't have to live according to the desires of the flesh, and we can live according to God's will revealed in the Bible. Christ enables us by His grace to do that. We are freed in Christ to live obedient lives even if that results in suffering for doing what is right.

In our case it is unlikely that we are going to suffer physically for doing what's right, but we might suffer emotionally, we might suffer socially, we might be shunned by society. And Peter says that is good — because that means you are following Jesus Christ, if you are suffering for righteousness' sake.

## We Have Already Wasted Enough Time Sinning (Verses 3-4)

Verses 3 and 4: "For we have spent enough of our past lifetime in doing the will of the Gentiles, when we walked in lewdness, lusts, drunkenness, revelries, drinking parties, and abominable idolatries. In regard to these, they think it strange that you do not run with them in the same flood of dissipation, speaking evil of you."

Peter, writing to a church that was mostly saved out of the Gentile world, says that they have wasted enough time collectively doing the things that were consistent with living in darkness. Now these people had been part of the Gentile culture, and though they may not have been involved in all these sins, they were immersed in a culture that did. But where they came from does not have to dictate who they are now — that is the point Peter is making.

Notice that Peter says the Gentiles are out there, and they are not part of them anymore. The people in the church of Jesus Christ are no longer Gentiles. They are no longer the sinners out there — even though the people 1 Peter is written to were ethnically Gentiles, because through their union with Christ they had become part of a new people, the Israel of God. As Paul says, if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation — old things have passed away, behold, all things have become new. Who you were outside of Christ does not dictate who you are in Christ.

This is very important because we often succumb to the idea that who we were and what happened to us determine who we are — that we cannot escape that. That if we came from whatever family of origin, we are doomed to repeat the same mistakes. That if we suffered some trauma, that trauma will identify us and define who we are. And Peter says: no. You were once something; now you are a new creation in Jesus Christ. You were Gentiles, and now in Christ you are no longer Gentiles — therefore you do not have to act like one of them.

Peter provides a non-exhaustive list of what Gentiles do. There are no exhaustive lists of sins in the Bible — because if the Bible gave us a complete list, we would say, "Great, now I can do the other one I just invented." So Peter gives us a non-exhaustive list. Six activities that are characteristic of people who are not united to Christ:

Lewdness — behavior completely lacking moral restraint, just doing whatever you want. By now you might know I like Hallmark movies. And one of the themes of Hallmark movies is "follow your heart." Peter says no — have your behavior restrained by the Word of God. That's the opposite of lewdness.

Lusts — not necessarily the word for sexual desire specifically, but inordinate desires. Wanting something more than you want God.

Drunkenness — literally losing your self-control because of substances. Not just wine, but any substance that will alter your mind that you choose to recreationally consume.

Revelries and drinking parties — social practices of the Gentile world organized around debauchery.

Abominable idolatries — disgusting idolatrous behavior that even civil government would forbid.

Paul calls all these practices in verse 4 "a flood of dissipation or debauchery." Interesting that he uses the word flood, because in chapter 3 he used that word for the means by which God judged the world. In their dissipation, they are going to be judged the same way that generation in Noah's time was judged by the waters of the flood.

Do you notice what these six activities have in common? Two things: they are all based on a lack of self-control, and they are all self-centered. The follower of Jesus Christ has been changed. These things that may have characterized him before do not characterize him or her anymore, because they walk in the Spirit. So the list that applies to us is not 1 Peter 4:3 but Galatians 5:22-23: "The fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, longsuffering, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, self-control."

Let me hasten to say: you do not have to have practiced these evil things in order to not practice them anymore. I don't want to leave you with the idea that unless you've practiced these things, you can't repent of them. If you grew up in the church and in Christ and haven't lived in overt rebellion, praise the Lord for that. That's the glorious testimony we want to hear from everyone.

Now notice that the Christian life faithfully lived is a stark contrast to the world. In verse 4, Peter says the people see you, they knew that you were part of them, and now you are not willing to go along with them anymore — and they speak evil of you. They blaspheme you for not following the norms of the culture around you. You're committing cultural heresy by not following what they do.

The faithful Christian will be condemned by the world because his life is a condemnation to the world. If a non-Christian never sees reason to think that you don't think like them and don't do what they do — and you have a close relationship with them — you need to examine your life.

## God Will Judge the Righteous and the Unrighteous (Verses 5-6)

Look at verses 5 and 6: "They will give an account to Him who is ready to judge the living and the dead. For this reason the gospel was preached also to those who are dead, that they might be judged according to men in the flesh, but live according to God in the spirit."

Peter encourages us to entrust ourselves to God because God will judge those who persecute us for virtue's sake in this life — that's what he means there by "the living" — and in the life to come, that's what he means there by "the dead" in verse 5. And here we are back to the theme of 1 Peter: entrust yourself to God. He will vindicate you. You don't have to worry about that.

The unbelievers who blaspheme you, who speak evil of you, will have to answer directly to God who will judge them for blaspheming His Son. Because when you are spoken evil of because of your godly behavior, Christ is being blasphemed.

And then for this reason in verse 6 — for this reason of judgment — the gospel was preached to people who were judged in the flesh by the world, but live in the spirit by God. The world brought death and condemnation; by God they are brought to life and God vindicates them. Do you want examples of that? Look at Christ's resurrection. Look at Noah's vindication through the flood. Look at your own baptism.

## Application: Relationships with Unbelievers

Christ equips us to obey God. We have already wasted enough time sinning. So let us not waste any more time sinning because God will judge both the righteous and the unrighteous. How does that look like tomorrow?

Brothers and sisters, you are united to Christ and transformed by Him. You can rely on Him to strengthen you to resist sin. You are able by His grace to choose to suffer instead of sinning.

I want you to think about how this truth affects your relationships with unbelievers. In a friendship with an unbeliever, there will be times where you will experience pushback for not going along with what they want to do. And if you have close relationships with unbelievers and never experience this pushback, these verses are encouraging you to examine how you live your life. It is not that God calls you to be a jerk to your unbelieving friends. But if you are living faithfully according to the will of God, there will be conflict with them.

There is also an application here to romantic relationships. If you are a believer seeking to live a faithful life, you will not be able to have a satisfying romantic relationship with an unbeliever — and what I mean by a romantic relationship is having a boyfriend or girlfriend who is an unbeliever. If you are married, guess what? You are married. The fact that your spouse is an unbeliever does not nullify your marriage. It will make for a difficult marriage but it is still marriage nonetheless.

I am not even saying that this passage specifically forbids you from dating an unbeliever, though the Bible does indirectly forbid it since dating has a looking-toward-marriage aspect to it and the Bible clearly forbids a believer from marrying an unbeliever. But what I am saying is that this passage teaches, among other things, that you will have a miserable romantic relationship with an unbeliever if you are living a faithful life to Christ. The foundational differences are too great — to pull a verse out of context but use the illustration — Amos 3:3: "Can two walk together unless they are agreed?"

So if you are dating an unbeliever and things are going peachy in your relationship, if there is no conflict, it most likely means that you are not living a life that is looking to Christ and being faithful to Him. You look at the Scriptures and without exception, when a believer enters an interrelationship, it is not the unbeliever who is brought up — it is the believer who is brought low. We are different than the world, and if in our most intimate relationships that is not evident, then there is a problem with our faith.

For all of us, brothers and sisters, we have been united with Christ. Let the life that we now live be His living through us.

Let us pray together. Father, thank you for your word. We thank you that it is powerful. We pray that it will not return void to you as you have promised but will accomplish your purposes. Point us to Christ even as we think about the things said here this morning. We ask in Jesus's name. Amen.
